incidents.title: Wandering Resident - Exit Attempt
incidents.description: Resident with dementia attempted to leave through emergency exit

questions.questionText: What security measures were in place?

answer.answerText: Door alarm was active. I was able to redirect the resident before he reached the door. He was looking for his wife.

questions.questionText: What was done to redirect the resident?

answer.answerText: I engaged him in conversation about his wife, showed him her photo in his room, and offered to call her. He calmed down and agreed to have lunch instead.

incidents.title: Skin Tear - Left Forearm

answer.answerText: Cleaned with saline, applied antibiotic ointment, covered with non-adherent dressing. Documented in wound log and photographed for tracking.

incidents.summary: Minor skin tear treated with proper wound care protocol. Family notified and satisfied with response.

humanReport.summary: During morning care, discovered a small skin tear on Dorothy Wilson's left forearm. Wound was cleaned per protocol, antibiotic ointment applied, and covered with non-adherent dressing. Daughter was notified immediately and expressed gratitude for prompt care.
humanReport.insights: Skin tear likely occurred during sleep due to fragile skin. No signs of trauma or external cause. Resident takes blood thinners which increases skin fragility.
humanReport.recommendations: Continue monitoring skin integrity during all care activities. Consider long-sleeve protection garments at night. Review blood thinner dosage with physician if skin tears become frequent.
humanReport.actions: Daily dressing changes for 7 days. Photograph healing progress. Family follow-up call scheduled for end of week.

incidents.title: Grant Hill Incident Report
incidents.description:
At approximately 8:40 PM, a "fall in room" alert was triggered by the bed alarm for resident Grant Hill (Room 308). The resident was discovered on the floor at the right side of his bed, lying on his left hip. He was attempting to reach his bedside table, where a tray with a snack and juice had been left.

Mr. Hill was trying to grab the juice cup, which was placed just out of comfortable reach. He overextended, lost his balance, and slid off the edge of the low-profile bed onto the floor mat.

questions.questionText: Describe what happened during the incident.

answer.answerText:
At approximately 8:40 PM, a "fall in room" alert was triggered by the bed alarm for resident Grant Hill (Room 308). The resident was discovered on the floor at the right side of his bed, lying on his left hip. He was attempting to reach his bedside table, where a tray with a snack and juice had been left.

Mr. Hill was trying to grab the juice cup, which was placed just out of comfortable reach. He overextended, lost his balance, and slid off the edge of the low-profile bed onto the floor mat.

questions.questionText: How is the resident doing right now?

answer.answerText:
Physical: Mr. Hill is a 78-year-old male with a history of osteoarthritis and lower-body weakness, which increases his fall risk. He was alert and oriented, complaining of pain in his left hip and elbow. He was wearing non-slip socks, but his pajama pants were slightly twisted around his legs.

Mental/Emotional: The resident was frustrated and embarrassed, stating, "I was just thirsty. I didn't want to bother anyone for a simple cup of juice. I thought I could get it." He has a known history of not using his call light for what he considers "minor" requests, preferring to be independent.

questions.questionText: Describe the room or environment conditions.

answer.answerText:
naccessible Personal Items: The primary cause was the poor placement of the resident's snack tray. The bedside table was positioned too far from the bed, requiring the resident to reach and stretch over the edge, creating a loss of balance.

Poor Lighting: The room was dimly lit, with only a small television on. The main overhead light was off, and the small nightlight near the bathroom was obscured by a privacy curtain, making it difficult for Mr. Hill to accurately judge the distance to the cup.

Room Clutter: While not the direct cause of the fall, the area around the bed was cluttered. A walker was present but folded against the wall, not within reach. An IV pole (not in use) was also positioned near the table, creating a tight, complex space for movement or recovery.

Equipment: The low-profile bed and floor mat functioned correctly. The mat cushioned the impact, but the bed's height, while low, still allowed the resident to slide off when overextending.

On the evening of the incident, at approximately 8:40 PM, a fall alert was triggered for resident Grant Hill in Room 308. Upon discovery, Mr. Hill was found on the floor beside his bed, lying on his left hip. He had attempted to reach for a juice cup placed on a tray at his bedside, which was positioned too far for comfortable access. In his effort to retrieve the cup, he overextended and lost his balance, resulting in the fall.

Mr. Hill, a 78-year-old male with a history of osteoarthritis and lower-body weakness, was alert and oriented but expressed pain in his left hip and elbow. He exhibited signs of frustration and embarrassment regarding the incident, indicating a preference for independence and a tendency to avoid using the call light for assistance. Environmental factors contributing to the fall included poor placement of personal items, dim lighting in the room, and clutter around the bed area, which complicated his movement.

Observations:

  • Resident was alert and oriented but in pain.
  • Bedside snack tray was poorly positioned, contributing to the fall.
  • Room lighting was inadequate, affecting visibility.
  • Clutter around the bed included a walker and an unused IV pole, limiting space for movement.

Next Steps:

  • Review and adjust the placement of personal items to ensure accessibility.
  • Improve room lighting to enhance visibility.
  • Consider a reassessment of the resident's independence preferences and fall risk management strategies.

incidents.title: Lina Cinder Incident Report
incidents.description:
At approximately 12:15 PM, during the main lunch service, resident Lina Cinder (a 74-year-old female, typically cooperative) had a severe verbal altercation with a dietary aide, Mark.

The incident was triggered when Mark served Ms. Cinder her plate (the standard chicken and vegetable option). Ms. Cinder looked at the plate, pushed it away, and loudly accused Mark of "bringing her poison."

When Mark attempted to de-escalate by saying, "Ms. Cinder, this is just the chicken you had last week," she became highly agitated. She began shouting, "Liar! You're all liars here! You're trying to get rid of me!" Her voice was loud enough to silence the entire dining hall, and she swiped at the plate, knocking a glass of water onto the floor.

The outburst lasted for approximately two minutes and caused several other residents at nearby tables to stop eating. Two residents appeared visibly anxious and distressed by the shouting. The lunch service was effectively "unbearable" and halted until a charge nurse was able to intervene, speak calmly to Ms. Cinder, and escort her back to her room to de-escalate.

questions.questionText: How is the resident doing right now?

answer.answerText:
Physical: Ms. Cinder was physically unharmed. During the episode, she was flushed, trembling, and breathing rapidly.

Mental/Emotional: This incident represents a significant and sudden change from her baseline behavior. While she has occasionally been forgetful, she has never shown signs of aggression or paranoia. Her belief that the staff was "trying to poison her" and that they were "all liars" points to acute paranoia and delusion. This uncharacteristic outburst, triggered by a routine event, is a strong indicator that warrants an immediate cognitive workup for the possible onset of dementia.

questions.questionText: Describe the room or environment conditions.

answer.answerText:
High-Stimulus Setting: The dining hall was at its busiest. The high level of ambient noise, movement, and number of people (approx. 25-30 residents plus staff) may have been an overwhelming sensory trigger for a resident experiencing cognitive decline.

Public Nature: The perceived "confrontation" (receiving her plate) happened in a public setting, which may have heightened her anxiety and defensive reaction.

Routine Disruption: While the meal was standard, it may not have been what Ms. Cinder was expecting or remembering, leading to a confused and fearful reaction that she could only express as an accusation.

On October 12, 2023, at approximately 12:15 PM, resident Lina Cinder, a 74-year-old female, experienced a significant behavioral incident during lunch service. The high-stimulus environment of the busy dining hall may have contributed to her overwhelming response, suggesting a potential cognitive decline that warrants further evaluation.

  • This behavior represents a significant change from her baseline, indicating possible cognitive issues.

Next Steps:

incidents.title: Gonzalo Ruban Incident Report
incidents.description:
At 1:55 PM, resident Gonzalo Riban, 81, was found on the floor in the main hallway outside the living room. He was en route to the 2:00 PM movie.

According to Mr. Riban, he dropped his glasses. Instead of using his call light, he unlocked his wheelchair brakes to reposition. He then leaned far forward and to the right to grab them, causing the unsecured chair to tip. He toppled out, landing hard on his left shoulder and hip.

incidents.title: Ricardo Montalban Incident Report
incidents.description:
At approximately 1:55 PM, resident Ricardo Montalban (81) was found on the floor in the main dining hall, just outside the living room. Lunch service was over, but several residents were watching a soccer match on the TV.

When his team (Brazil) won, Mr. Montalban, a passionate fan, became ecstatic. He pushed his walker aside, shouted, "Goal!" and attempted a celebratory salsa spin. He lost his balance during the turn and fell forward, striking his face and right shoulder on the hard tile floor.

questions.questionText: Describe what happened during the incident.

answer.answerText:
At approximately 1:55 PM, resident Ricardo Montalban (81) was found on the floor in the main dining hall, just outside the living room. Lunch service was over, but several residents were watching a soccer match on the TV.

When his team (Brazil) won, Mr. Montalban, a passionate fan, became ecstatic. He pushed his walker aside, shouted, "Goal!" and attempted a celebratory salsa spin. He lost his balance during the turn and fell forward, striking his face and right shoulder on the hard tile floor.

questions.questionText: How is the resident doing right now?

answer.answerText:
Physical: Mr. Montalban was found with a laceration on his upper lip. He was complaining of severe mouth pain. Upon assessment, his upper-right incisor was avulsed (knocked out). He also reported new, sharp pain in his right shoulder.

Mental/Emotional: The resident's elation immediately turned to anger and grumpiness. He was irritable with staff ("Leave me alone!") and highly distressed about his appearance, specifically his lost tooth, stating, "My smile is ruined! I look like a fool!" He refused to stay in the dining hall and was assisted to his room to rest and await dental consultation.

questions.questionText: Describe the room or environment conditions.

answer.answerText:
Improper Use of Assistive Device: The resident is care-planned to use his walker for all ambulation. He deliberately pushed his walker away to dance, creating an immediate and high-risk fall situation.

Slippery Surface: The dining hall floor is smooth tile. While not wet, it offers very little friction, especially for the leather-soled dress shoes Mr. Montalban insists on wearing.

Situational Risk: The "celebration" itself was a behavioral risk factor. The sudden, high-energy, and complex physical activity (salsa spinning) was far beyond his known physical limitations and balance issues.

incidents.title: Clara Jenkins Incident Report
incidents.description:
At 9:10 AM, resident Clara Jenkins (84) slipped and fell in the hallway directly outside her room. A dietary aide had just cleaned up a spilled water pitcher and was returning to the cart. The "Wet Floor" sign was in place.

Ms. Jenkins, who has documented glaucoma and walks with a cane, exited her room, reportedly to go to the activities lounge. She did not see the sign, stepped onto the still-damp tile, and her feet went out from under her. She fell backward, landing on her left hip and attempting to brace herself with her right hand.

questions.questionText: Describe what happened during the incident.

answer.answerText:
At 9:10 AM, resident Clara Jenkins (84) slipped and fell in the hallway directly outside her room. A dietary aide had just cleaned up a spilled water pitcher and was returning to the cart. The "Wet Floor" sign was in place.

Ms. Jenkins, who has documented glaucoma and walks with a cane, exited her room, reportedly to go to the activities lounge. She did not see the sign, stepped onto the still-damp tile, and her feet went out from under her. She fell backward, landing on her left hip and attempting to brace herself with her right hand.

questions.questionText: How is the resident doing right now?

answer.answerText:
Physical: Found on her back, crying out. She did not lose consciousness. She has a known history of osteoporosis. She is reporting severe pain in her right wrist and hip. A small skin tear is visible on her right forearm.

Mental/Emotional: The resident is highly anxious and confused about the event. She keeps repeating, "I didn't see it! I didn't see the water. Why was it wet?" Her anxiety is high due to a known fear of falling.

questions.questionText: Describe the room or environment conditions.

answer.answerText:
Poor Signage Placement: The "Wet Floor" sign was placed, but it was set parallel to the wall, next to the spill, not in the direct path of egress from the resident's doorway.

Visual Hazard: The floor is a high-glare beige tile, making it extremely difficult for a resident with glaucoma to perceive the clear, damp patch.

Lack of Direct Warning: The aide was present but did not provide a verbal warning as Ms. Jenkins exited her room, assuming she would see the sign.

aiReport.insights:
### Insights:

1. **What happened?**
   - John Deer was found in the dining room after slipping while reaching for a Coke.
   - It is believed that he was wearing old slippers that did not provide adequate grip on the floor, contributing to the slip.
   - The dining hall environment was reportedly not wet or slippery, indicating that the footwear was the primary issue.

2. **What happened to the resident?**
   - John is reported to be in great spirits following the incident, suggesting no serious injury.
   - He expressed concern about the need to purchase new slippers, indicating awareness of the potential hazard posed by his current footwear.

3. **How could we have prevented this incident?**
   - Implement a footwear policy for residents that prohibits the use of worn or inappropriate footwear, emphasizing the need for non-slip shoes in communal areas.
   - Regularly assess and educate residents about the importance of wearing appropriate footwear, especially in dining and common areas.
   - Conduct routine checks of resident footwear and provide guidance or assistance in selecting appropriate options.

4. **What should we do to stop incidents like this in the future?**
   - Establish a protocol for regular safety checks in communal areas, including evaluating the condition of flooring and ensuring it remains free of hazards.
   - Increase staff vigilance in monitoring residents, especially those who may be at higher risk of falls due to improper footwear.
   - Provide training for staff on fall prevention strategies and encouraging proactive engagement with residents about their safety and footwear choices.
   - Consider installing non-slip mats or signage in dining areas as a preventive measure against slips and falls.
aiReport.recommendations:
**Recommendations:**

1. **Staff Training Improvements:**
   - **Conduct Regular Training Sessions:** Implement mandatory training for all staff on fall prevention and safety protocols, focusing on the importance of appropriate footwear for residents. Include scenarios that emphasize identifying unsafe footwear and how to intervene when residents are at risk of slipping.
   - **Incorporate a Slip and Fall Prevention Module:** Develop a dedicated module within the training program that addresses slip hazards, including environmental assessments and resident mobility assistance techniques.

2. **Environmental/Equipment Changes:**
   - **Install Non-Slip Flooring:** Evaluate the dining hall flooring and consider adding non-slip mats or coatings to reduce the risk of slips and falls. Ensure that any new flooring materials meet safety standards for communal areas.
   - **Provide Proper Footwear:** Establish a supply of appropriate footwear that residents can use while in communal areas. This could include non-slip slippers or shoes, ensuring that residents have access to safe options.

3. **Policy or Protocol Updates:**
   - **Create a Footwear Policy:** Develop and implement a policy that outlines acceptable footwear for residents in communal areas. This policy should specify the types of footwear that are considered safe and encourage regular checks for wear and tear.
   - **Incident Reporting Protocol:** Revise the incident reporting policy to ensure that all slips and falls are documented with specific details regarding resident footwear and environmental conditions, allowing for trend analysis and improved safety measures.

4. **Monitoring or Documentation Enhancements:**
   - **Regular Safety Audits:** Schedule routine safety audits of communal areas to identify potential slip hazards, including flooring conditions, cleanliness, and resident footwear compliance. Document findings and address issues promptly.

5. **Engagement and Feedback Loop:**
   - **Resident Feedback Sessions:** Organize regular feedback sessions with residents to discuss safety concerns, including footwear issues. Encourage residents to share their experiences and suggestions for improvement, fostering a culture of safety awareness.

initialReport.enhancedNarrative:
Resident Name: Lyle Wright

Summary: On the morning of the incident, resident Lyle Wright was found on the floor after experiencing a fall. Prior to the fall, Lyle had consumed apple cider and reported a sudden need to use the restroom during the night. It is believed that the mat in the room may not have been stable, contributing to the incident. Lyle was observed to have urine on his clothing, indicating a possible loss of control prior to the fall. Upon entering the room to assist Lyle for breakfast, staff discovered him on the floor.

Lyle's overall condition appears stable, although he may be experiencing psychological distress due to the embarrassment associated with the incident. Environmental factors, including the potential for slippage from the mat and the socks Lyle was wearing, should be assessed to prevent future occurrences.

Observations:

  • Resident found on the floor with urine on clothing.
  • Possible contributing factors: unstable mat and slippery socks.
  • Resident's psychological state may be affected by the incident.

Next Steps:

  • Evaluate the stability of floor mats in resident rooms.
  • Consider providing non-slip socks for residents.
  • Monitor Lyle's emotional well-being and provide support as needed.

incidents.title: Gerard Beaubrun - Bed Fall -  Incident Report
incidents.description:
At 8:40 PM, resident Gerard Bubbler's bed alarm was activated, prompting staff to respond. Upon arrival, Gerard was found on the floor mat adjacent to his bed, which was positioned correctly at a low height. He appeared alert but expressed frustration, indicating that he had reached for his juice when he fell. Gerard has a history of osteoarthritis and exhibits poor trunk control, which may have contributed to the incident. He was wearing non-slip socks at the time and reported pain in his left hip and right wrist, while denying any head injury.

The environmental assessment revealed that the room was dimly lit, with only the television providing illumination. The bedside table was positioned too far from the bed, and the call light had fallen to the floor, making it inaccessible. These factors likely contributed to Gerard's overextension while attempting to reach for his juice, resulting in his fall.

Observations:

  • Resident was alert but frustrated post-fall.
  • Reported pain in left hip and right wrist; no head injury noted.
  • Bed alarm activated prior to the incident.
  • Non-slip socks were worn.

Environmental Factors:

  • Room was dimly lit with only the TV on.
  • Bedside table was too far from the bed.
  • Call light was on the floor, out of reach.
  • Walker was folded and placed against the wall.

Next Steps:

  • Assess and address the placement of the bedside table and call light for improved accessibility.
  • Monitor Gerard's pain levels and provide appropriate interventions.
  • Review fall prevention strategies with staff to enhance resident safety.

questions.questionText: Describe what happened during the incident.

answer.answerText: At 8:40 PM, gerard's bed alarm sounded. I found him on the floor mat on the right side of his bed. The bed was in the correct low position, and the floor mat was in place, which cushioned his fall.

questions.questionText: How is the resident doing right now?

answer.answerText: He was alert but frustrated, stating, 'I was just thirsty and reaching for my juice.' He has known osteoarthritis and poor trunk control. He was wearing non-slip socks. He is reporting pain in his left hip and right wrist. He denies hitting his head.

questions.questionText: Describe the room or environment conditions.

answer.answerText: The room was dim, with only the TV on. His walker was folded by the wall. The main issue was the bedside table was placed too far away, and his call light had fallen on the floor, just out of reach. He overextended for the juice and slid off the bed.

questions.questionText: Was the bedside table within reach for Gerard at the time of the fall?

answer.answerText: Based on our internal review, the table was placed at the standard 18-inch distance mandated by facility protocol. The problem is that Mr. Hill’s care plan, which requires a **hospital-style overbed table**, was not being followed. His arthritis flare-ups, which were noted that morning, significantly reduce his functional reach. So, while the table was "to code," it was *not* accessible for this specific resident in his documented condition.

questions.questionText: Were there any other assistive devices available to Gerard besides the walker?

answer.answerText: Yes, his file shows he was prescribed a 32-inch **reacher/grabber tool** specifically for this purpose. When we interviewed the resident, he stated he "couldn't find it" in the dim light. It was later found hanging on his closet doorknob, which is not the correct, accessible placement. Staff have been reminded to secure it to the bedrail clip.

questions.questionText: Did Gerard call for assistance before attempting to reach for his juice?

answer.answerText: No. This is a known behavioral issue with Mr. Hill. He has repeatedly stated in his care conferences that he "doesn't want to be a bother" and will wait until he is "desperate" to use his call light. This "stoicism" is a major risk factor we are trying to address with him and his family.

questions.questionText: Was the call light functional and easily accessible prior to the fall?

answer.answerText: The call light itself is functional. However, the resident’s room has older model beds, and the call light cord is not long enough to be safely clipped to his gown (our preferred method). It was clipped to his bedsheet, and we believe it was pulled onto the floor when he repositioned himself earlier. He was unable to retrieve it from the floor.

questions.questionText: How frequently has Gerard reported issues with reaching for items in the past?

answer.answerText: Mr. Hill himself rarely reports it. However, his **roommate has complained on his behalf four times** in the last two months. These complaints were logged by the evening shift but were never escalated to a formal fall-risk meeting. This represents a significant gap in our communication protocols.

questions.questionText: What measures are in place to ensure adequate lighting in the resident's room during evening hours?

answer.answerText: Facility policy is a passive, low-level nightlight. However, Mr. Hill **refuses to use it**, as he claims it "shines in my eyes and keeps me awake." He has signed a waiver to this effect. He prefers the room to be dark, with only the TV on, which actually creates poor depth perception. This is a "resident's rights" issue that conflicts directly with our safety measures.

questions.questionText: Have there been any recent changes to Gerard's care plan or mobility assistance?

answer.answerText: **Yes.** Just last week, he was prescribed a new diuretic (water pill) to be taken in the evenings. This has predictably increased his thirst and need to use the restroom at night. The fall-risk assessment should have been *immediately* updated to reflect this medication change, but it appears this was missed. The increased thirst was a direct contributor to this incident.

questions.questionText: Were staff aware of Gerard's frustration or discomfort prior to the fall incident?

answer.answerText: It's not documented in the nurse's notes, but the CNA assigned to his wing was a temporary "agency" staff member. Mr. Hill is known to be "agitated" by unfamiliar caregivers and is less likely to ask them for help. It's very likely he was frustrated by the new face and made the decision to get the juice himself rather than interact with a stranger.

aiReport.summary: On November 10, 2025, at 8:40 PM, resident Gerard Beaubrun was found on the floor mat next to his bed after his bed alarm was activated. He had attempted to reach for his juice, leading to his fall, while experiencing pain in his left hip and right wrist. Contributing factors included dim lighting, the bedside table being out of reach, and the call light being inaccessible on the floor, alongside his history of osteoarthritis and poor trunk control.
aiReport.insights:
### Insights:

1. **What happened?**
   - Gerard Beaubrun's bed alarm was activated at 8:40 PM, indicating he may have needed assistance.
   - Upon staff arrival, he was found on the floor mat adjacent to his bed after attempting to reach for his juice.
   - Contributing factors included dim lighting, an inaccessible bedside table, and a fallen call light.

2. **What happened to the resident?**
   - Gerard was alert but frustrated, reporting pain in his left hip and right wrist, while denying any head injury.
   - His pre-existing conditions, including osteoarthritis and poor trunk control, may have contributed to the fall.
   - The incident increased his pain and highlighted gaps in accessibility and communication regarding his needs.

3. **How could we have prevented this incident?**
   - Ensure the bedside table is positioned within reach, in accordance with Gerard's specific care plan requirements.
   - Keep the call light securely within reach and ensure it is functional and accessible at all times.
   - Increase room lighting during evening hours or provide alternative low-light options that do not disturb the resident.
   - Regularly review and update fall-risk assessments, especially following changes in medication or health status.

4. **What should we do to stop incidents like this in the future?**
   - Implement staff training focused on communication and observation skills to better understand residents' needs and preferences.
   - Establish a protocol for timely updates to fall-risk assessments following changes in medication or health conditions.
   - Create a more robust system for documenting and escalating resident complaints about their care environment.
   - Engage residents and families in discussions about safety measures, addressing concerns about rights versus safety, particularly regarding lighting and call light accessibility.
aiReport.recommendations:
**Recommendations:**

1. **Staff Training Improvements:**
   - Implement mandatory training sessions focused on fall prevention strategies, particularly addressing the needs of residents with mobility issues and cognitive or behavioral challenges. Include role-playing scenarios to improve staff responses to resident needs and enhance communication skills, especially when dealing with residents who may be hesitant to ask for help.
   - Conduct regular workshops on the importance of following individual care plans and the impact of medication changes on resident behavior and fall risk. Emphasize the need for thorough documentation and communication about residents' conditions and concerns, especially when transitioning staff.

2. **Environmental/Equipment Changes:**
   - Reconfigure the layout of Gerard's room to ensure that the bedside table is within easy reach and install a hospital-style overbed table as specified in his care plan. Ensure that all residents with similar mobility issues have their furniture arrangements assessed and modified as necessary.
   - Upgrade the call light system to include longer cords that can be easily clipped to the resident’s gown, ensuring they remain accessible. Additionally, consider providing bedside lights that can be adjusted to avoid shining directly in the resident's eyes while still offering adequate illumination.

3. **Policy or Protocol Updates:**
   - Revise the fall risk assessment protocol to require immediate updates following any changes in medication or health status. Establish a standardized process for notifying staff of such changes, ensuring that all caregivers are aware of the current care plan and any additional needs.
   - Develop a policy requiring regular interdisciplinary team meetings to discuss residents’ needs, particularly concerning behavioral issues and communication challenges. Include family members in these discussions when appropriate to foster a collaborative approach to care.

4. **Monitoring or Documentation Enhancements:**
   - Implement a standardized incident reporting tool that includes sections for documenting environmental factors, resident behavior, and staff observations before and after incidents. This will help identify patterns and facilitate proactive measures.
   - Introduce a system for tracking resident complaints or concerns voiced by roommates or family members, ensuring that they are escalated to care teams promptly. This can help in addressing potential issues before they lead to incidents.

5. **Enhanced Accessibility Tools:**
   - Ensure that assistive devices, such as the reacher/grabber tool, are secured in easily accessible locations. Create a checklist for staff to verify that all necessary assistive devices are available and within reach for residents, and conduct regular audits to ensure compliance.

  - **Action:** Move the bedside table closer to Gerard's bed to ensure it is within easy reach.
  - **Responsible Party:** Facility Manager
  - **Due Date:** By end of shift on 11/11/2025

- **Secure Call Light Accessibility**
  - **Action:** Ensure the call light is secured to Gerard's bedrail clip and within reach, and replace the cord with a longer model if necessary.
  - **Responsible Party:** Nursing Director
  - **Due Date:** By end of shift on 11/11/2025

- **Implement Lighting Solutions**
  - **Action:** Assess and install an alternative lighting solution (e.g., a low-profile nightlight) that meets Gerard's preferences while ensuring safety.
  - **Responsible Party:** Facility Manager
  - **Due Date:** By 11/12/2025

- **Update Fall-Risk Assessment**
  - **Action:** Review and update Gerard's fall-risk assessment to reflect recent changes in his care plan, including the new diuretic prescription and mobility assistance needs.

- **Conduct Staff Training on Communication and Fall Prevention**
  - **Action:** Organize a training session for staff on effective communication strategies with residents, especially those exhibiting stoic behavior, and review fall prevention protocols.

These action items are designed to address immediate concerns following the incident with Gerard Beaubrun, improve safety measures, and enhance staff awareness regarding fall prevention and effective communication with residents.

initialReport.enhancedNarrative:
On the date of the incident, resident Linda Glover experienced an emotional outburst during lunch, during which she accused another resident, Mark, of 'poisoning' her. This outburst led to her knocking over a glass of water, causing a disruption among other residents at the table. In the course of the incident, Linda fell forward and struck her head on the table. A nurse subsequently escorted her to her room for further evaluation.

Upon assessment, Linda was found to have a cut on her head and an injury to her left wrist. She reported feeling dizzy and stated that she had no recollection of the fall or her behavior towards Mark. At the time of the incident, Linda was dressed in a dress, wearing a scarf on her head, and had a bracelet on her left wrist. She was also wearing slippers, which may have contributed to her stability during the incident.

Observations:

  • Resident experienced an emotional outburst during lunch.
  • Knocked over a glass of water, causing disruption.
  • Fell forward, hitting her head on the table.
  • Injuries noted: cut on head and injury to left wrist.
  • Resident reported dizziness and lack of memory regarding the incident.

Next Steps:

  • Monitor resident for any changes in condition.
  • Document injuries and provide appropriate care.
  • Consider a review of resident's emotional support needs.
  • Evaluate dining environment for safety improvements.
